review: I recently watched Alex Garland's film Ex Machina, who is also known for films such as Sunshine and Annihilation. The premise of the film is a young programmer tasked with determining whether an advanced AI possesses true consciousness, and it explores the interesting philosophical dimensions of artificial intelligence and consciousness. In general Garland is able to build an interesting atmosphere and the cinematography is excellent. However, I found that the movie is a bit too slow in the beginning and the characters are only explored at surface level, which personally left me wanting for a bit more. The issues the movie tackles are also quite simplified, possibly to make the film a bit more digestible, but I personally felt that a few less clichés would have improved the film. Reviewed by Jr Robot 123

Blade Runner 2049 is a masterclass in sci-fi filmmaking. The director managed to create a sequel that stands on its own while honoring the original. The visuals are breathtaking, every frame could be a painting. Ryan Gosling delivers a subtle, understated performance that fits the tone perfectly. My only complaint is the runtime, at nearly three hours, it does test your patience in places. But if you appreciate slow burn cinema with incredible world-building, this is essential viewing. 9/10.

just watched parasite last night and wow joon ho is a genius. it totally caught me off guard in ways i didnt expect. went in knowing nothing and i think thats the best way to experiece it. my girlfriend was literally shaking at the end. if you havent seen it yet what are you even doing. easily top 5 movies ive ever seen

The Shawshank Redemption (1994). I finally got around to watching this after years of it being at the top of every 'best movies' list. And honestly? It lives up to the hype. Tim Robbins and Morgan Freeman have incredible chemistry. The pacing is perfect, even at 2+ hours it never drags. The ending is one of the most satisfying I've ever seen in cinema. I understand now why this is considered one of the greatest films ever made. Frank Darabont adapted Stephen King's novella beautifully.

Watched Tenet last weekend. I have now watched it three times and I still don't fully understand what's happening. Nolan has officially disappeared up his own complexity. The sound mixing is atrocious, couldn't hear half the dialogue. John David Washington is charismatic but the script gives him nothing to work with emotionally. Robert Pattinson is the best part. It looks gorgeous and the action sequences are genuinely inventive, but a movie shouldn't require a PhD to follow. Disappointed.

I took my kids to see Inside Out 2 and ended up crying more than they did lol. Pixar really knows how to hit you right in the feelings. The new emtions especially Anxiety were so well done and relatable. My 8 year old turned to me and said "dad thats you when you check your email" when Anxiety showed up. Animation quality is top notch as always. Only downside is some of the humor felt a bit recylced from the first one. Still a solid 4 out of 5 stars from the whole family.

I absolutely hated Everything Everywhere All at Once. I know this is an unpopular opinion and everyone treats this movie like the second coming but I found it exhuasting and tryhard. Yes the multiverse concept is creative. Yes Michelle Yeoh is talented. But the constant visual chaos gave me a headache and the emotional beats felt manipulitive rather than earned. Some of the alternate universe stuff was just stupid. Fight me in the comments. 2/10

The Grand Budapest Hotel, Wes Anderson at his most Wes Anderson. If you like his style you'll love it, if you don't you won't, simple as that. Ralph Fiennes is absolutely hilarious as M. Gustave. The production design is immaculate. Every shot is composed like a postcard. It's funny, surprisingly moving at times, and the ensemble cast is stacked. I've rewatched it probably six times and notice new details every time. One of my comfort movies.

Oppenheimer was fine I guess? Like it was well made and Cillian Murphy was great but I felt like the movie couldn't decide if it wanted to be a character study or a courtroom drama or a war movie. The non-linear timeline was confuisng for no reason. Also three hours is too long for any movie I don't care who directed it. The Trinity test scene was admitedly incredible though. Emily Blunt deserved more screen time. It's not bad but I don't get the best picture hype.

INTERSTELLAR IS THE GRETEST MOVIE EVER MADE AND I WILL DIE ON THIS HILL. The docking scene alone is worth the price of admission. Hans Zimmer's score is TRANSEDENT. There are scenes in this movie that make me literally sob every single time. Yes some of the themes get a bit cheesy but I don't care. Nolan made a movie about a dad who just wants to get home to his kids and wrapped it in the most ambitous scifi spectacle of the decade. MASTERPIECE!!! 11/10 would cry again
